elements.sent-2411-001: A generative adversarial network (GAN) has been a representative backbone model in generative artificial intelligence (AI) because of its powerful performance in capturing intricate data-generating processes.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, the GAN training is well-known for its notorious training instability, usually characterized by the occurrence of mode collapse.
elements.sent-2411-003: Through the lens of gradients' variance, this work particularly analyzes the training instability and inefficiency in the presence of mode collapse by linking it to multimodality in the target distribution.
elements.sent-2411-004: To ease the raised training issues from severe multimodality, we introduce a novel GAN training framework that leverages a series of tempered distributions produced via convex interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-005: With our newly developed GAN objective function, the generator can learn all the tempered distributions simultaneously, conceptually resonating with the parallel tempering in Statistics.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our simulation studies demonstrate the superiority of our approach over existing popular training strategies in both image and tabular data synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-007: We theoretically analyze that such significant improvement can arise from reducing the variance of gradient estimates by using the tempered distributions.
elements.sent-2411-008: Finally, we further develop a variant of the proposed framework aimed at generating fair synthetic data which is one of the growing interests in the field of trustworthy AI.. \thispagestyle{empty.}
elements.sent-2411-009: The rising demand for bigger data with privacy has led to the widespread adoption of data generators (or synthesizers) across various domains \citep{jord:etal:22 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: For instance, the European General Data Protection Regulation mandates data deletion after its primary purpose and restricts sharing due to ownership and privacy concerns.
elements.sent-2411-011: As a promising solution, \cite{mott:18 employed a generative model to synthesize Passenger-Name-Records (PNR) data while discarding the original one to comply with such a data protection policy.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Meanwhile, large-scale AI models, devouring massive training datasets, exploit the generative model's capability to produce an arbitrary number of data points for efficient training \citep{hwan:etal:23 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In the literature, the generative adversarial network \citep[GAN,][]{good:etal:14 particularly stands out as a versatile data synthesizer, demonstrating exceptional capabilities in reconstructing diverse datasets, such as images \citep{kang;etal;23 , text \citep{de;etal;21 , tabular data \citep{zhao:etal:21 , and even estimating model parameters \citep{wang;etal;22 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The GAN framework consists of two competing networks $D\in {\cal D}$ (i.e., the critic) and $G\in{\cal G}$ (i.e., the generator) where $\cal D$ and $\cal G$ have neural-net families.
elements.sent-2411-015: Let $X\in {\cal X = \mathbb{R ^{d_X}$ and $Z\in {\cal Z = \mathbb{R ^{d_Z}$ be random variables with density $p_X$ and $p_Z$ respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: We denote by $G:\mathbb{R ^{d_Z \rightarrow \mathbb{R ^{d_X}$ the generator that ideally transforms the reference variable $Z$ to be $G(Z)\overset{d {= X$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The essence of learning (or estimating) $G$ starts from approximating a divergence $d(p_X,p_{G(Z) )$ between two probability distributions by ${\cal D}$, i.e., $d(p_X,p_{G(Z) ) \approx d_{\cal D (p_X,p_{G(Z) )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Then it finds the optimal $G$ satisfying $d_{\cal D (p_X,p_{G(Z) )=0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Under this general framework, diverse probability metrics have been attempted such as the Jensen-Shannon divergence \citep[JSD,][]{good:etal:14 , the 1-Wasserstein distance \citep{arjo:etal:17 , $f$-divergence \citep{nowo:etal:16 , and so forth.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Despite its great potential as a high-quality data synthesizer, GAN has been known to be brutally unstable and easily fall into nonconvergence because of its min-max (or adversarial) optimization structure.
elements.sent-2411-021: To resolve this training issue, there has been a plethora of research discovering better training tricks or network architectures, mostly based on empirical findings and specifically for image data synthesis \citep{jabb:etal:21 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: From a more fundamental perspective, \cite{mesc:etal:18 suggested penalizing the average gradient norm of $D$, so that there would be no power to break the equilibrium between $D$ and $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-023: To see more relevant studies, refer to \cite{roth:etal:17,gulr:etal:17 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Tempering (or smoothing) $p_X$ has also been studied as an effective strategy to stabilize the GAN training mainly for the information-based divergence.
elements.sent-2411-025: They show that expanding supports of $X$ and $G(Z)$ by adding $\epsilon$ can enable $D$ and $G$ to yield meaningful and stabilized gradients.
elements.sent-2411-026: Based on this notable study, \cite{sajj:etal:18 attempted annealing a functional noise of $X$ created by an auxiliary neural network during the GAN training with JSD.}
elements.sent-2411-027: While the existing studies benefit from resolving the unstable optimization issue of JSD associated with support mismatch, this work discovers a stable training mechanism by tempering a multimodal $p_X$ via a convex interpolation scheme in GAN with the approximated 1-Wasserstein distance.
elements.sent-2411-028: Based on this novel discovery of smoothing $p_X$ in GAN training, we eventually devise an efficient and stable GAN framework by learning multiple levels of tempered distributions simultaneously, so it is called the parallelly tempered generative adversarial networks (PTGAN).
elements.sent-2411-029: Section~\ref{sec:mech explains the estimation mechanism of the GAN training and discusses a source of training instability focusing on characterizing the gradients' variance of the critic.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Section~\ref{sec:tempered_dist defines the convex interpolation that creates a tempered density of $p_X$ and analyzes how it alleviates the multimodality of $p_X$.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Section~\ref{sec:parallel specifically designs the proposed framework that effectively incorporates the interpolation scheme without an annealing schedule.}
elements.sent-2411-032: While our approach inherently possesses a bias-variance trade-off in updating the critic, the learned GAN model achieves the nearly optimal minimax rate.
elements.sent-2411-033: Lastly, Section~\ref{sec:simul verifies that PTGAN significantly outperforms popular competing models in various benchmark data sets.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Moreover, we extend PTGAN for a fair data generation task to which trustworthy AI has paid great attention recently.
elements.sent-2411-035: To our knowledge, this is the first work to theoretically analyze the GAN mechanism through the lens of the gradients' variance of $D$ with the tempered density of $p_X$.
elements.sent-2411-036: To define a target distance metric $d_{\cal D}$, let's consider families of fully-connected neural networks: ${\cal D = \{D(x) = w^{\top _d \kappa_{d-1 (W_{d-1}\kappa_{d-2 (\cdots W_1 x)) : \bw=(W_1,\dots, W_{d-1 , w_d)\in \bW\}$ and ${\cal G =\{G(z) = v^{\top _g \psi_{g-1 (V_{g-1}\psi_{g-2 (\cdots V_1 z)) : \bv=(V_1,\dots, V_{g-1 , v_g)\in \bV\}$, where $w_d\in \mathbb{R ^{p_{d}\times 1}$, $v_d\in \mathbb{R ^{q_{g}\times d_X}$, $W_i \in \mathbb{R ^{p_{i+1}\times p_i}$ for $i=1,\dots,d-1$, $V_j \in \mathbb{R ^{q_{j+1}\times q_j}$ for $j=1,\dots,g-1$, $p_1 = d_X$, and $q_1 = d_Z$; $\kappa_i$ and $\psi_j$ are element-wise non-linear activation functions.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Then the neural distance \citep{aror:etal:17 is defined as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-039: For a given monotone concave function $\phi:\mathbb{R \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, $G\in {\cal G}$, and $D \in {\cal D}$, $d_{\cal D (p_X,p_{G(Z) )=\sup_{D \in {\cal D \bE (\phi(D(X))) + \bE (\phi(1-D(G(Z))))$ is called a neural distance between $X$ and $G(Z)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The specification of $\phi$ determines the type of discrepancy.
elements.sent-2411-041: With $\phi(x)=\log(x)$ and the sigmoid output of $D$, $d_{\cal D}$ approximates JSD.
elements.sent-2411-042: If $\phi(x)=x$, the 1-Wasserstein distance is approximated via the Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality.
elements.sent-2411-043: This work particularly focuses on the identity mapping of $\phi$, which leads to the approximated 1-Wasserstein distance by $D$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:nd} d_{\cal D}(p_X,p_{G(Z)})=\sup_{D \in {\cal D}} \bE (D(X)) - \bE (D(G(Z))).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: For this target distance, the optimal critic function $D^*$ is the maximizer of $L(D,G)=\bE(D(X))-\bE(D(G(Z)))$ such that $d_{\cal D (p_{X ,p_{G(Z) )=\expect (D^*(X))-\expect (D^*(G(Z)))$, and the optimal generator $G^*$ is the minimizer of $d_{\cal D (p_X,p_{G^*(Z) )$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-059: To estimate $D^*$ and $G^*$, the iterative gradient-based estimation alternates gradient ascent/descent at the $t$th iteration, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{grad_desc} \bw^{(t+1)}=\bw^{(t)} + \gamma_D \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \bw^{(t)}} \hat{L}_b(D^{(t)},G^{(t)}),\quad \bv^{(t+1)}=\bv^{(t)} - \gamma_G \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \bv^{(t)}} \hat{L}_b(D^{(t+1)},G^{(t)}),\end{equation} where $D^{(t)}$ and $G^{(t)}$ (or $\bw^{(t)}$ and $\bv^{(t)}$) are the $t$th iterate of $D$ and $G$, and $\gamma_D$ and $\gamma_G$ are learning rates.

elements.sent-2411-083: Our analysis begins by observing mode collapse gives rise to multimodality between $\Gt$ and $\calS^c$.
elements.sent-2411-084: Keeping in mind this relationship, we first demonstrate that the neural distance is a well-suited metric for assessing multimodality, and then clarify that the degree of multimodality is closely associated with the size of gradients' variance.
elements.sent-2411-085: In Statistics, $p_X$ is called a multimodal distribution when its density function has more than one peak.
elements.sent-2411-086: For example, $p_X$ that consists of two unimodal distributions $p_k$ for $k=1,2$ whose mean and variance are $\mu_k$ and $\sigma_k^2$ shapes a bimodal density expressed by $p_{X (x)=\sum_{k=1 ^2 p_k(x;\mu_k,\sigma_k^2)/2$.}}

elements.sent-2411-089: Likewise, the neural distance represents the degree of bimodality as shown in the following Proposition~\ref{prop:nd_wd , but it mainly reflects the between-variability when equal variances are given.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Under (A1-3), the neural distance between probability distributions $p_1(x;\mu_1,\sigma^2)$ and $p_2(x;\mu_2,\sigma^2)$ in a location-scale distribution family is bounded by \begin{equation}|\mu_1-\mu_2|-\delta_{\cal D} \leq d_{\cal D}(p_1,p_2) \leq \min\left\{\prod_{l=1}^d M_w(l) \prod_{s=1}^{d-1} K_{\kappa}(s)|\mu_1-\mu_2|, |\mu_1-\mu_2|+\delta_{\cal D}\right\},\end{equation} where $\delta_{\cal D}$ stands for the universal approximation capability of $\cal D$, i.e., the neural networks in $\cal D$ can approximate any 1-Lipschitz function with $\delta_{\cal D}$ error on a compact support (please refer to Supplementary~\ref{supp:prop1 for a more concrete explanation of $\delta_{\cal D}$).}

elements.sent-2411-293: This work primarily focuses on fair data generation that has recently seen a growing demand for morality control in machine learning models.
elements.sent-2411-294: In algorithmic fairness, a key focus aims to minimize discrimination by decision models, denoted as $h$, against minor groups.
elements.sent-2411-295: We pay attention to a classification problem; $C\in {\cal C}$ represents covariates, $A\in\{0,1\}$ is a binary sensitive attribute (e.g., race or gender), $Y\in \{0,1\}$ is a binary outcome, and $h:{\cal C}\rightarrow [0,1]$ is learned to predict $Y$.
elements.sent-2411-296: The goal is to ensure statistical independence between $h(C)$ and $A$, so $h$ returns fair outcomes regardless of $A$.
elements.sent-2411-297: Discrimination is measured by $|\bE(\hat{Y |A=1)-\bE(\hat{Y |A=0)|$ where $\hat{Y =1(h(C)>\tau)$ for some $\tau$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-298: This measurement, called statistical/demographic parity (SP), tends to compromise the model's utility, such as classification accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-299: For more details and recent studies on algorithmic fairness, see \cite{baro:etal:17,sohn:etal:23 and reference therein.}
elements.sent-2411-300: Interestingly, the PTGAN framework can be used to enable $G$ to produce various levels of fair synthetic data while holding the training stability.

elements.sent-2411-322: In this work, we demystify how mode collapse stirs the inflation of the gradients' variance of the critic function by investigating the impact of the remaining neural distance toward missing support.
elements.sent-2411-323: As an explicit remedy that shortens the remaining distance, PTGAN builds the bridge connecting all modes in $p_X$ via the convex interpolation.
elements.sent-2411-324: We theoretically justify that the proposed framework enjoys the variance reduction effects of gradients.
elements.sent-2411-325: Rather than struggling to achieve the state-of-the-art scores of IS/FID or AUC on various challenging data sets, our work concentrates on clarifying the training improvement by leveraging tempered target distributions with the minimal use of extra training tricks.
elements.sent-2411-326: Finally, we introduce the fair data generation task as an intriguing variant of PTGAN that addresses the importance and necessity of the generator's capability to produce intermediate distributions beyond enhancing training stability.

elements.sent-2411-459: The scores are calculated as follows: \begin{equation}IS =\exp\left( \bE_{X\sim p_{G(Z)}}(KL(p(y|X)||p(y))) \right), FID =||\mu_X - \mu_G||_2^2 + tr\left(\Sigma_X + \Sigma_{G} -2 \left(\Sigma_X^{\frac{1}{2}}\Sigma_{G} \Sigma_X^{\frac{1}{2}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}\right),\end{equation} where $\mu_X = \bE(\tilde{y (X))$, $\mu_G = \bE(\tilde{y (G(Z)))$, $\Sigma_X = \cov(\tilde{y (X))$, and $\Sigma_G = \cov(\tilde{y (G(Z)))$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-460: For IS, the lower the entropy of $p(y|G(Z))$ is, the higher fidelity images $G$ produces.
elements.sent-2411-461: The marginal probability $p(y)=\int p(y|G(z))p(G(z))p(z)dz$ having higher entropy implies more diversity.
elements.sent-2411-462: Therefore, as the discrepancy of the KL divergence increases, it can be said that the generator achieves higher performance on both the high quality of images and the diversity.
elements.sent-2411-463: On the one hand, FID measures the distance in the latent space under the assumption that the latent feature follows multivariate Gaussian distributions.

elements.sent-2411-533: Despite its great potential as a high-quality data synthesizer, GAN has been known to be brutally unstable and easily fall into non-convergence because of its min-max (or adversarial) optimization structure.
elements.sent-2411-534: To resolve this training issue, a plethora of research has discovered better training tricks or network architectures, mostly based on empirical findings and specifically for image data synthesis \citep{jabb:etal:21 .}
elements.sent-2411-535: They show that expanding supports of $X$ and $G(Z)$ by adding $\epsilon$ can remedy the gradient instability induced by the support mismatch between $X$ and $G(Z)$, thus enabling $D$ and $G$ to yield meaningful gradients.
elements.sent-2411-536: Based on this study, \cite{sajj:etal:18 attempted to anneal a functional noise of $X$ created by an auxiliary neural network during the GAN training with JSD.}
elements.sent-2411-537: While the existing studies benefit from resolving the unstable optimization issue associated with support mismatch when using JSD, this work discovers a stable training mechanism by tempering a multimodal $p_X$ via a convex interpolation scheme in GAN with the scaled 1-Wasserstein distance.
elements.sent-2411-538: Based on this novel discovery of smoothing $p_X$ in GAN training, we eventually devise an efficient and stable GAN framework by learning multiple levels of tempered distributions simultaneously, so it is called parallelly tempered generative adversarial networks (PTGAN).
elements.sent-2411-539: Section~\ref{sec:mech explains the estimation mechanism of the GAN training and discusses a source of training instability, focusing on characterizing the gradients' variance of $D$.}
elements.sent-2411-540: To our knowledge, this is the first work to theoretically analyze the GAN mechanism through the lens of the gradients' variance of $D$.
elements.sent-2411-541: Section~\ref{sec:parallel specifically designs the proposed framework that incorporates the interpolation scheme without an annealing schedule.}
elements.sent-2411-542: While our approach inherently possesses a bias-variance trade-off in updating the critic, our model achieves the nearly optimal minimax rate.

elements.sent-2411-546: To define a target distance metric $d_{\cal D}$, let's consider families of fully connected neural networks: ${\cal D = \{D(x) = w^{\top _d \kappa_{d-1 (W_{d-1}\kappa_{d-2 (\cdots W_1 x)) : \bw=(W_1,\dots, W_{d-1 , w_d)\in \bW\}$ and ${\cal G =\{G(z) = v^{\top _g \psi_{g-1 (V_{g-1}\psi_{g-2 (\cdots V_1 z)) : \bv=(V_1,\dots, V_{g-1 , v_g)\in \bV\}$, where $w_d\in \mathbb{R ^{N^D_d\times 1}$, $v_g\in \mathbb{R ^{N^G_g\times d_X}$, $W_i \in \mathbb{R ^{N^D_{i+1}\times N^D_i}$ for $i=1,\dots,d-1$, $V_j \in \mathbb{R ^{N^G_{j+1}\times N^G_j}$ for $j=1,\dots,g-1$, $N^D_1 = d_X$, and $N^G_1 = d_Z$; $\kappa_i$ and $\psi_j$ are element-wise non-linear activation functions.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-547: For simplicity, the above representations of ${\cal D}$ and ${\cal G}$ don't explicitly include bias nodes, as bias nodes can be induced by augmenting the network input by a constant.
elements.sent-2411-548: The neural distance \citep{aror:etal:17 is defined as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-549: For a given monotone concave function $\phi:\mathbb{R \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, $G\in {\cal G}$, and a network class ${\cal D}$, $d_{\cal D (p_X,p_{G(Z) )=\sup_{D \in {\cal D \{\bE [\phi(D(X))] + \bE [\phi(1-D(G(Z)))]\}$ is called a neural distance between $X$ and $G(Z)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-550: This work considers $\phi(x)=x$, leading to the scaled 1-Wasserstein distance which is approximated by \begin{equation}\label{eq:nd} d_{\cal D}(p_X,p_{G(Z)})=\sup_{D \in {\cal D}} \{\bE[D(X)] - \bE[D(G(Z))]\},\end{equation} via the Kantorovich-Rubinstein duality.
elements.sent-2411-551: Note that for $d_{\cal D}$ to approximate JSD, one specifies $\phi(x)=\log(x)$ and the sigmoid output of $D$.
elements.sent-2411-552: For \eqref{eq:nd , the optimal critic function $D^*$ is the maximizer of $L(D,G)=\bE[D(X)]-\bE[D(G(Z))]$ such that $d_{\cal D (p_{X ,p_{G(Z) )=\expect [D^*(X)]-\expect [D^*(G(Z))]$, and the optimal generator $G^*$ is the minimizer of $d_{\cal D (p_X,p_{G(Z) )$.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-562: To estimate $D^*$ and $G^*$, we alternate gradient ascent/descent: \begin{equation}\label{grad_desc} \bw^{(t+1)}=\bw^{(t)} + \gamma_D \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \bw}\hat{L}_b(D^{(t)},G^{(t)}),\quad \bv^{(t+1)}=\bv^{(t)} - \gamma_G \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \bv}\hat{L}_b(D^{(t+1)},G^{(t)}),\end{equation} where $D^{(t)}$ and $G^{(t)}$ (or $\bw^{(t)}$, $\bv^{(t)}$) are $t$th iterates and $\gamma_D$, $\gamma_G$ are learning rates.

elements.sent-2411-755: This work focuses on fair data generation, addressing the growing demand for morality control in machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-756: In algorithmic fairness, the goal is to reduce discrimination by decision models $h$ against certain subpopulations.
elements.sent-2411-757: We consider a classification setting with covariates $C \in {\cal C}$, binary sensitive attribute $A \in \{0,1\}$ (e.g., race or gender), and binary outcome $Y \in \{0,1\}$, where $h:{\cal C \to [0,1]$ predicts $Y$.}
elements.sent-2411-758: Fairness requires statistical independence between $h(C)$ and $A$, ensuring $h$ is unaffected by $A$.
elements.sent-2411-759: Discrimination is quantified by $\lvert\bE[\hat{Y |A=1] - \bE[\hat{Y |A=0]\rvert$ with $\hat{Y = 1(h(C) > \tau)$, known as statistical or demographic parity (SP), though enforcing SP often compromises utility such as accuracy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-760: See \cite{baro:etal:17, sohn:etal:23 for more details and recent advances.}
elements.figure-2411-027: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{image/pareto_util_fair_tradeoff.png} \caption{The trade-off curves between AUC and SP for the downstream classifiers.} \label{fig:uftradeoff}
elements.sent-2411-761: Our FairPTGAN framework is verified by comparing the behavior of trade-offs between utility and fairness to a recent fair generative model \citep[FairWGANGP,][]{raja:etal:22 and a seminar preprocessing method \citep[GeoRepair,][]{feld:etal:15 .}}
elements.sent-2411-762: FairWGANGP places a penalty term $\lambda_f \lvert\bE[\tilde{Y |\tilde{A =1]-\bE[\tilde{Y |\tilde{A =0]\rvert$ when updating $\Gt$ where $(\tilde{C ,\tilde{A ,\tilde{Y ) \sim \Gt(Z)$, so that $\Gt$ produces societally unbiased synthetic data.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-763: GeoRepair solves a Wasserstein-median problem between $C|A=1$ and $C|A=0$ with a weighting parameter $0\leq \lambda_p \leq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-764: Following the same evaluation procedure in tabular data generation, the Pareto frontiers are found by evaluating AUC and SP of the downstream models on the remaining 10\% test data.
elements.sent-2411-765: FairPTGAN is computationally efficient and achieves favorable Pareto frontiers.
elements.sent-2411-766: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:fair_comp , it attains lower statistical parity (SP) than competitors at certain utility levels.}
elements.sent-2411-767: Our joint learning structure in \eqref{builtin enables the generator to produce datasets with different fairness levels by simply varying $\alpha$ in $\Gt(Z, \alpha)$.}
elements.sent-2411-768: In contrast, FairWGANGP and GeoRepair require retraining or repeated processing when their fairness parameters $\lambda_f$, $\lambda_p$ change.
elements.sent-2411-769: While GeoRepair is model-free, its computational cost gets brutally expensive as the number of entities and variables in the data increases.

elements.subsection-2411-024: Training stability affected by gradients variance on $\Dt$
elements.sent-2411-782: This section, we aims to empirically justify the importance of gradient variance of $\Dt$ by showing that a large gradient variance of $\Dt$ during the training can lead unstable GAN training.
elements.sent-2411-783: We choose the 8-mixture toy example (Figure~\ref{fig:mode_collapse ) and explicitly control the gradient variance by injecting additional Gaussian noise ${\rm N (0,\sigma^2)$ to each gradient of $\Dt$ during training (Figure~\ref{fig:noisy_gradients ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-784: To heighten the effect of increasing gradient variance, this experiment is conducted for the Lipschitz GAN \citepSupp{zhou:etal:22 .}
elements.sent-2411-785: Note that the Lipschitz GAN pursues more stable training than the original GAN by encouraging the critic function $\Dt$ to be Lipschitz via adding a gradient penalty, and it shows successful performance on the toy example when no additional noise is injected to the gradient.
elements.sent-2411-786: The experiment runs the GAN model 10 times independently.
elements.sent-2411-787: For each run, we evaluate the 1-Wasserstein distance\footnote{We use the Python library ``ot" to calculate the 1-Wasserstein distance approximately.}
elements.sent-2411-788: As shown in the figure, the GAN training with $\sigma=0.01$ shows a significantly fluctuating performance among 10 independent runs, which implies that GAN training with high variance of gradients on the critic can undergo difficult optimization for the generator.
elements.sent-2411-789: This experiment intentionally magnifies the variance of the $\Dt$'s gradients to observe that GAN training with high variance of gradients can fail.
elements.sent-2411-790: In practice, we hypothesize that there may be various sources causing high variance of gradients, such as mode collapse, misspecified hyperparameters, overfitting, etc..

Shaded areas represent one standard deviation from the average lines at every iteration. Note $\sigma$ stands for the size of the standard deviation of Gaussian noise added to the gradients of $\Dt$ during the training.} \label{fig:noisy_gradients}

elements.sent-2411-934: Recall that $\delta^{d-1 _{x =1\times w_d \odot \kappa_{d-1 '(B_{d-1 (x))$ and that $\delta^l_{x =(W_{l+1 ^{\top}\delta^{l+1 _{x )\odot \kappa_{l '(B_l(x))$ where $B_l(x)\in {\mathbb R ^{N_{l+1 ^D}$ (Section~\ref{supp:backprop ).}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-935: To simplify the discussion, we define a diagonal matrix $T_l(x)=\diag(\kappa_{l '(B_{l,1 (x)),\dots ,\kappa_l'(B_{l,N_{l+1 ^D (x)))\in \mathbb{R ^{{N_{l+1 ^D}\times {N_{l+1 ^D }$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-936: Then it appears that \begin{equation}\delta^l_{x}=(W_{l+1}^{\top}\delta^{l+1}_{x})\odot \kappa_{l}'(B_l(x))=T_l(x)\cdot W^{\top}_{l+1}\cdot \delta_{x}^{l+1},\end{equation} which means that $\delta^l_{x,r = T_{l,r,r (x)\times (W_{l+1 ^{\top}\delta_x^{l+1 )_r$ where $({\bf x )_r$ is the $r$th component of the vector ${\bf x}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-937: Through the recursive structure of $\delta^l_x$, we observe \begin{equation}\delta^{d-1}_x =T_{d-1}(x)\cdot w_d, \delta^{d-2}_x =T_{d-2}(x)\cdot W_{d-1}^{\top} \cdot T_{d-1}(x)\cdot w_d, \delta^{d-3}_x =T_{d-3}(x)\cdot W_{d-2}^{\top} \cdot T_{d-2}(x)\cdot W_{d-1}^{\top}\cdot T_{d-1}(x)\cdot w_d, \vdots\end{equation} The $r$th component presents $\delta^{l _{x,r =T_{l,r,r (x)\times W_{l+1,r ^{\top}\cdot v$ where $W_{l+1,r ^{\top}$ is the $r$th row vector of $W^{\top _{l+1}$, and $v$ is the output of the remaining products of the matrices and vector.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-938: In the next paragraphs, we will characterize the size of the gradient of $w_d$, $W_{d-1}$, and $W_{d-2}$, which is then generalized to the $l$th layer.
elements.sent-2411-939: The gradient of $w_{d,r}$ is \begin{equation}\dfrac{\pr \hat{L}_b}{\pr w_{d,r}} = \dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b} A_{d-1,r}(X_i)-A_{d-1,r}(G(Z_i)),\end{equation} so by Lemma~\ref{lemma:dist_uwb , \begin{equation}\left\lvert \dfrac{\pr \hat{L}_b}{\pr w_{d,r}} \right\rvert \leq \dfrac{1}{M_w(d)}d_{\cal D}(p_X,p_{G(Z)})+O_p(1/\sqrt{n_b}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-940: Before characterizing the size of this gradient, we first make the following remarks.
elements.sent-2411-941: Under (A1-3), the function $A_{l,c (x)$ is bounded as discussed in Remark~\ref{rem:boundA (when $\kappa(0)=0$), i.e., \begin{equation}|A_{l,c}(x)| \leq K_{\kappa}(l)\lVert W_{l,c} \rVert \prod_{j=1}^{l-1}K_{\kappa}(j)\lVert W_{j} \rVert_F\lVert x \rVert, \leq K_{\kappa}(l)\lVert W_{l,c} \rVert C_{A_{l-1}}\times \max\left\{B_X,B_{G(Z)}\right\},\end{equation} where $B_{G(Z) =\prod_{j=1 ^g M_v(j) K_{\psi (j) B_Z$ and set $C_{A_{l-1 =\prod_{s=1 ^{l-1 K_{\kappa (s)\lVert W_{s \rVert_F$.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-129: \begin{equation}|A_{l,c}(x)| \leq K_{\kappa}(l)\lVert W_{l,c} \rVert \prod_{j=1}^{l-1}K_{\kappa}(j)\lVert W_{j} \rVert_F\lVert x \rVert, \leq K_{\kappa}(l)\lVert W_{l,c} \rVert C_{A_{l-1}}\times \max\left\{B_X,B_{G(Z)}\right\},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-942: Note $B_X$ and $B_{G(Z)}$ stand for the size of the domain ${\cal X}$ and $\{G(z):z\in {\cal Z}\}$.
elements.sent-2411-943: We also remark that some activation functions, such as Sigmoid or Tanh, are trivially bounded.
elements.sent-2411-944: For Lipschitzness, the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality derives \begin{equation}|A_{l,c}(x)-A_{l,c}(y)| =|\kappa_l(W_{l,c}A_{l-1}(x))-\kappa_l(W_{l,c}A_{l-1}(y))|, \leq K_{\kappa}(l) \lVert W_{l,c}\rVert \lVert A_{l-1}(x)-A_{l-1}(y) \rVert, \leq K_{\kappa}(l)\lVert W_{l,c} \rVert \prod_{s=1}^{l-1}K_{\kappa}(s)\lVert W_{s} \rVert_F \times \lVert x - y\rVert, = K_{\kappa}(l)\lVert W_{l,c} \rVert \times C_{A_{l-1}}\times \lVert x-y\rVert.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-130: \begin{equation}|A_{l,c}(x)-A_{l,c}(y)| =|\kappa_l(W_{l,c}A_{l-1}(x))-\kappa_l(W_{l,c}A_{l-1}(y))|, \leq K_{\kappa}(l) \lVert W_{l,c}\rVert \lVert A_{l-1}(x)-A_{l-1}(y) \rVert, \leq K_{\kappa}(l)\lVert W_{l,c} \rVert \prod_{s=1}^{l-1}K_{\kappa}(s)\lVert W_{s} \rVert_F \times \lVert x - y\rVert, = K_{\kappa}(l)\lVert W_{l,c} \rVert \times C_{A_{l-1}}\times \lVert x-y\rVert.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-945: We define constants $M_{A_{l,c }$ and $L_{A_{l,c }$ such that \begin{equation}|A_{l,c}(x)|\leq M_{A_{l,c}}, |A_{l,c}(x)-A_{l,c}(y)|\leq L_{A_{l,c}} \lVert x-y\rVert,\end{equation} for all $x$ and $y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-946: If $\kappa_{l '(x)$ is $K_{\kappa' (l)$-Lipschitz, it follows that \begin{equation}|\kappa'_{l}(B_{l,r}(x))-\kappa'_{l}(B_{l,r}(y))| =|\kappa'_{l}(W_{l,r}A_{l-1}(x))-\kappa'_{l}(W_{l,r}A_{l-1}(y)| \leq K_{\kappa'}(l)|W_{l,r}A_{l-1}(x)-W_{l,r}A_{l-1}(y))| \leq K_{\kappa'}(l)\lVert W_{l,r} \rVert \lVert A_{l-1}(x)-A_{l-1}(y)\rVert \leq K_{\kappa'}(l)\lVert W_{l,r} \rVert \times C_{A_{l-1}}\times \lVert x -y\rVert.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-132: \begin{equation}|\kappa'_{l}(B_{l,r}(x))-\kappa'_{l}(B_{l,r}(y))| =|\kappa'_{l}(W_{l,r}A_{l-1}(x))-\kappa'_{l}(W_{l,r}A_{l-1}(y)| \leq K_{\kappa'}(l)|W_{l,r}A_{l-1}(x)-W_{l,r}A_{l-1}(y))| \leq K_{\kappa'}(l)\lVert W_{l,r} \rVert \lVert A_{l-1}(x)-A_{l-1}(y)\rVert \leq K_{\kappa'}(l)\lVert W_{l,r} \rVert \times C_{A_{l-1}}\times \lVert x -y\rVert.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-947: For the boundeness, $|\kappa'_{l (x)|\leq K_{\kappa (l)$ for all $x$.}}
elements.sent-2411-948: We define constants $M_{\kappa'_{l,r }$ and $L_{\kappa'_{l,r }$ such that \begin{equation}|\kappa'_{l}(B_{l,r}(x))|\leq M_{\kappa'_{l,r}}, |\kappa'_{l}(B_{l,r}(x))-\kappa'_{l}(B_{l,r}(y))|\leq L_{\kappa'_{l,r}} \lVert x-y\rVert,\end{equation} for all $x,y$, and also for any $l,r$.}}
elements.sent-2411-949: Now, the backpropagated gradient can be decomposed as \begin{equation}\dfrac{1}{w_{d,r}}\times \dfrac{\pr \hat{L}_b}{\pr W_{d-1,r,c}}= \dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b}\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)A_{d-2,c}(X_i)- \kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i))A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i)) = \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b}\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)A_{d-2,c}(X_i)- \kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))}_{(I)} +\underbrace{\dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b}(\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)-\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i)))A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))}_{(II)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-950: For (I), \begin{equation}{(I)}= \dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b}\bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)]\left\{A_{d-2,c}(X_i)-A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))\right\} + \dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b}(\kappa'_{d-1,r}(X_i)-\bE[\kappa'_{d-1,r}(X_i))])\left\{A_{d-2,c}(X_i)-A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))\right\},\end{equation} where the Chebyshev's inequality approximates the second term \begin{equation}\dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b}(\kappa'_{d-1,r}(X_i)-\bE[\kappa'_{d-1,r}(X_i))])\left\{A_{d-2,c}(X_i)\pm\bE[A_{d-2,c}(X_i)]-A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))\right\}, = Cov(\kappa'_{d-1,r}(X_i), A_{d-2,c}(X_i))+ O_p(1/\sqrt{n_b}),\end{equation} which conceptually represents the degree of alignment between the $r$th and $c$th nodes.
elements.sent-2411-951: In the meantime, (II) can be approximated by \begin{equation}(II)= \bE[(\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)-\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i)))A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))] + O_p(1/\sqrt{n_b}) = \bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)]\bE[A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))]-\bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i))A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))]+ O_p(1/\sqrt{n_b}) = \bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)]\bE[A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))]-\bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i))]\bE[A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))] +\bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i))]\bE[A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))]-\bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i))A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))]+ O_p(1/\sqrt{n_b}) = \bE[A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))](\bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)]-\bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i))]) -Cov(\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i)),A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i)))+ O_p(1/\sqrt{n_b}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-952: Likewise, we can decompose the gradient as \begin{equation}\dfrac{1}{w_{d,r}}\times \dfrac{\pr \hat{L}_b}{\pr W_{d-1,r,c}}= \dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b}\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)A_{d-2,c}(X_i)- \kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i))A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i)) = \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b}\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i))A_{d-2,c}(X_i)- \kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i))A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))}_{(I)} +\underbrace{\dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b}(\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)-\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i)))A_{d-2,c}(X_i)}_{(II)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-953: Following the previous approach, we have \begin{equation}{(I)}= \dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b}\bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i))]\left\{A_{d-2,c}(X_i)-A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))\right\} -Cov(\kappa'_{d-1,r}(G(Z_i)),A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i)))+O_p(1/\sqrt{n_b}),\end{equation} and \begin{equation}(II)= Cov(\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i),A_{d-2,c}(X_i)) +\bE[A_{d-2,c}(X_i)](\bE[\kappa'_{d-1,r}(X_i)]-\bE[\kappa'_{d-1,r}(G(Z_i))])+O_p(1/\sqrt{n_b}).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-955: By Lemma~\ref{lemma:dist_uwb , \begin{equation}\dfrac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b}\left\{A_{d-2,c}(X_i)-A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))\right\}\leq \dfrac{1}{\prod_{j=d-1}^d M_w(j)C_{\kappa'}(j)}d_{\cal D}(p_X,p_{\Gt(Z)})+O_p(1/\sqrt{n_b}),\end{equation} where $C_{\kappa' (j)$ disappears if ReLU activation is used.}}

elements.sent-2411-959: Let's define the technical constants \begin{equation}C'_{\kappa,1}(d-1) =\left\lvert \dfrac{\bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(X_i)]+\bE[\kappa_{d-1,r}'(G(Z_i))]}{2}\right \rvert, C_{\kappa,2}(d-1) =\left\lvert \dfrac{\bE[A_{d-2,c}(X_i)]+\bE[A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))]}{2}\right\rvert\times  L_{\kappa'_{d-1,r}},~\text{$\kappa'_{d-1}(x)$ is Lipschitz}, 2K_{\kappa}(d-1),~\text{$\kappa'_{d-1}(x)$ is bounded},  C_{\kappa,3}(d-1) =\lvert Cov(\kappa'_{d-1,r}(X_i),A_{d-2,c}(X_i))-Cov(\kappa'_{d-1,r}(G(Z_i)),A_{d-2,c}(G(Z_i))) \rvert.\end{equation}.

elements.algorithm-2411-006: \caption{Parallelly Tempered Generative Adversarial Nets}\label{alg:ptgan} \KwData{$\{x_i\}_{i=1}^n$ be a set of training data set. Set the training iteration $T$ and for the inner loop $T'$, the minibatch size $n_b=m_b$, $t=0$, the penalty size $\lambda$, the ratio $r$, the learning rate $\gamma_D$ and $\gamma_G$, and initialize $\bw^{(0)}$ and $\bv^{(0)}$.} \KwResult{$\bv^{(T)}$} { \While{$t \leq T$}{ Set $t' = 0$ and $t = t + 1$; /\% Create Minibatch (Algorithm~\ref{alg:minibatch} or~\ref{alg:fair-minibatch}) \%/ $\{q_{(i)}^{(1)}\}_{i=1}^{n_b}$, $\{q_{(i)}^{(2)}\}_{i=1}^{n_b}$, $\{\tilde{q}_{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{n_b}$, $\{\alpha_{(i)}^{(1)}\}_{i=1}^{n_b}$, $\{\tilde{\alpha}_{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{n_b}$~=~MC($\{x_i\}_{i=1}^{n}$); Generate $\{z_{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{n_b}$ from $p_{Z}$, \While{$t' < T'$}{ $t' = t' + 1$; /\% Evaluate the loss and penalty \%/ $\hat{L}_b(\bw^{(t)},\bv^{(t)}) = \frac{1}{n_{b}}\sum_{i=1}^{n_{b}}D_{\bw^{(t)}}(q_{(i)}^{(1)},\alpha_{(i)}^{(1)})-D_{\bw^{(t)}}(G_{\bv^{(t)}}(z_{(i)},\alpha_{(i)}^{(1)}),\alpha_{(i)}^{(1)})$; $\hat{H}(\bw^{(t)})=\frac{1}{n_b}\sum_{i=1}^{n_b} \left(\nabla_{\tilde{q}_{i}}D_{\bw^{(t)}}(\tilde{q}_{i},\tilde{\alpha}_{(i)})\cdot (q^{(1)}_{(i)}-q^{(2)}_{(i)})\right)^2$; /\% Update $\Dt$ \%/ $\bw^{(t+1)} = \bw^{(t)} + \gamma_D \frac{\partial}{\partial \bw}\left(\hat{L}_b(\bw^{(t)},\bv^{(t)}) - \lambda \hat{H}(\bw^{(t)})\right)$; } $\hat{L}_b^G(\bw^{(t+1)},\bv^{(t)})=-\frac{1}{n_{b}}\sum_{i=1}^{n_{b}}D_{\bw^{(t+1)}}(G_{\bv^{(t)}}(z_{(i)},\alpha_{(i)}^{(1)}),\alpha_{(i)}^{(1)})$; /\% Update $\Gt$ \%/ $\bv^{(t+1)} = \bv^{(t)} - \gamma_G \frac{\partial}{\partial \bv} \hat{L}_b^G(\bw^{(t+1)},\bv^{(t)})$; }}

elements.sent-2411-1129: The scores are calculated as follows: \begin{equation}IS =\exp\left( \bE_{X\sim p_{G(Z)}}(KL(p(y|X)||p(y))) \right), FID =\lVert \mu_X - \mu_G\rVert_2^2 + tr\left(\Sigma_X + \Sigma_{G} -2 \left(\Sigma_X^{\frac{1}{2}}\Sigma_{G} \Sigma_X^{\frac{1}{2}}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}\right),\end{equation} where $\mu_X = \bE(\tilde{y (X))$, $\mu_G = \bE(\tilde{y (G(Z)))$, $\Sigma_X = \cov(\tilde{y (X))$, and $\Sigma_G = \cov(\tilde{y (G(Z)))$.}}}}
elements.paragraph-2411-025: Fine-tuning procedure to calculate IS/FID scores
elements.sent-2411-1130: The pre-trained InceptionV3 model is fine-tuned for {\bf BloodMnist and {\bf CelebA-HQ by updating the weight and bias parameters in later layers (after the 249th layer) of the model.}}
elements.sent-2411-1131: After the global pooling layer, a dropout and a linear layer are placed whose size matches the output's dimension of each learning objective.
elements.sent-2411-1132: For BloodMnist, the parameters are updated to minimize the cross-entropy based on eight different labels via the Adam optimizer and stopped by the early stopping process.
elements.sent-2411-1133: Then, we calculate IS/FID scores using this fine-tuned Inception model.
elements.sent-2411-1134: For CelebA-HQ, the model minimizes the sum of cross-entropy losses, each of which measures the discrepancy between a facial attribute and its corresponding probability.
elements.sent-2411-1135: This is one simple way to execute multi-label learning to make the model figure out all facial attributes simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-1136: Other learning procedures are the same with BloodMnist.
elements.sent-2411-1137: For CelebA-HQ, we report two FID scores where the first is based on the original pre-trained Inception model but the second on the fine-tuned model.
elements.sent-2411-1138: Note that reporting the first-type FID is usual in the literature.
elements.paragraph-2411-026: Simulation setup for CIFAR10 and BloodMnist
elements.sent-2411-1139: For ours, the temperature $\alpha_i$ is concatenated to every hidden layer.
elements.sent-2411-1140: For $p_x$, CIFAR10 and BloodMnist have 32 and 64.
elements.sent-2411-1141: The total number of iterations $T$ is set to both 100k with minibatches having 100 data instances for each data set.
elements.sent-2411-1142: Referring to \citeSupp{zhou:etal:19 , the Adam optimizer's hyperparameters \citepSupp{king:ba:14 are set to $\beta_1=0.0$ and $\beta_2=0.9$ with the learning rates for $D$ and $G$ as 0.0001.}}
elements.sent-2411-1143: The spectral normalization layer (SN) is applied only to the original competitor \citepSupp{miya:etal:18 .}
elements.sent-2411-1144: For PTGAN, the generator uses the interpolated uniform variables based on the discussion in \ref{supp:inter_noise while competitors use the uniform distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-1145: IS/FID scores are measured at 10 different $t$ points that equally space the total number of iterations $T$ since the evaluation of IS and FID is computationally heavy.

elements.sent-2411-1153: We particularly draw the generated images of PTGAN, SNGAN, and Lipschitz GAN (LGAN) where all methods are trained under the neural distance (ND).
elements.sent-2411-1154: From our view, PTGAN and Lipschitz GAN are both partially successful in producing recognizable pictures in CIFAR10 while SNGAN seems not.
elements.sent-2411-1155: In BloodMnist, SNGAN even shows mode collapse, i.e., generating similarly looking blood cells.
elements.sent-2411-1156: For CelebA-HQ, PTGAN and LGAN have a similar level of visual quality from human perspective but their performance is separated by the Inception model.
elements.sent-2411-1157: We acknowledge that the CNN-based generator adopted in Figures~\ref{appen:cnn_spec and \ref{appen:cnn_spec_celeba may not be large enough to learn the semantic details of all the modalities and may also need many more training iterations with additional training tricks such as scheduling learning rate, weight decay, classifier guided generative modeling, doubling feature maps, etc. However, to adapt to our limited computational resources and also to see the pure effects of the proposed method, this work uses the relatively light network to conduct extensive comparisons.}}
